speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Sam Steinberg, Beverly Hills, California. Mr. Steinberg, so now you've met your beautiful wife, Selma. And where did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 28, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1948. And how did you start your life together? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me back off, basically. As I say, I came through [INAUDIBLE]. They helped me out by paying the rent. After six months I-- and I guess I was always independent. I just quit school, found a job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, I was seeking a job, trying to do. I went to the Federation, and they gave me aptitude tests. I didn't know what to do. I never had a profession. I never had anything that-- that I was able to deal. It said that I'm mechanically inclined somehow. Again, Selma's parents had friends. One was a Viennese Jew, who at home was a-- had taxis, was in a taxi business. The other one was a German Jew, who at home was an attorney studying to be a judge. He came here before the war in 1939, ended up working in the shipyard, and after the shipyard, went into the electrical business. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this little kid or fellow, he says, looking for a job. He said, well, come to work for us. Just as good as anything else, so I went to work as electrician. I got seventy-five cents an hour. That was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '48, the beginning of '48, just before I got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ended up working for them for eight years, the same company. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- after-- there were-- I mean, when I started working, they only had one employee, and then there were three of us employees. Eventually they-- they went to work for schools. They-- they started working for LA School District, not for the district itself, but building schools. We did-- we did a lot of building schools, and that was our specialty. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After eight years-- in the meantime, there's another gentleman came in, and his name was Karl [? Frankel, ?] who also thought that he was an electrician, or worked for the railroad someplace. Anyway, he came to work. Met me, and he came to work for Alvarado Electric, and worked with them. And we went into business as a company. We formed our own company. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The company I worked for, what used to call Alvarado Electric. They were on Alvarado Street and 11th. They treat me always well, maybe because I tried to give them the best of my ability. And when I told them that I'm going to leave, offered me part of their business. But at that time, I have made a commitment already and-- but as I say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went into business for myself. And it takes-- after a few years, we matured in the business. And I enjoyed what-- the type of work that they were doing for deal. And I went into the same work for-- for public works and school work and became a direct competitor of theirs. But throughout our competitions, we were always friends. We were friends till they passed away. And I was honored to-- to-- job wasn't deal-- anyway, to be a pallbearer for [? Charlie ?] [? Meyer, ?] who was their attorney. He had two boys, and they called me, and said that would have been the father's wishes to do. So whatever relationship I had during the eight years must have been fair enough. In my business, we worked, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I say, we did a little bit of private work but the specialty was in public works. And again, I guess I was successful. I did-- I was always a hard worker. I-- I-- I worked with the men on the job. Just because I signed the check didn't make me any better than-- than they. I had the responsibility. But we were a successful company. I-- We had about six, seven employees. I had some differences with Karl [? Frankel, ?] and eventually ended up on myself. But again, we always remained friends. His philosophy was a little bit different than mine on certain things but-- and I continued. With him I was in KS Electric, and then I ended up as Sam's Electric, and continued in my business as a successful businessman in the trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your wife have how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have four kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest is Rachel, and she's about 42. Next is Steven, and he's 40. Next is Sharon, and she's 38. And then is Perry, and he's 36. Rachel has three kids, two girls and one boy. Steven has two girls. Sharon has two girls. And Perry has one girls. So I have eight grandkids, one-- seven granddaughters, one-- one grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now that it's 50 years after the war, let me ask you-- is there any [SIGHS] lesson or message that you'd like to give the people watching this video or to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Message? Advice is cheap. I have learned one thing-- never tolerate bigotry [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because it will come back to haunt you. If you think that bigotry is affecting other people, not you, we have definitely had a lesson through it. The trouble is that human nature, seeing through life in the 50 years, we have not learned that lesson. It is continuing. Second, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] try to be as generous as possible. Be giving. I have found by being involved in different organizations, being able to help, seeing in Israel and here, the accomplishments of institutions and things that were created by people giving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Give. Don't get discouraged if you read that there is x amount of waste by people who run those charitable organizations. There is waste, but there is creativity. Those things are created. I was minute helpful in creating the Holocaust Museum in Washington. Working through there helps. I am involved a little bit in [? Nashitorah ?]. I see what they do for the Jewish people. It helps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] IDL, federations, I belong to 1939 Club. I happen to be a past president. I don't know however I ended up there, but they somehow squeezed me in there. You raise x amount of money. We are membership of 700 people. First of all, it gives you, by belonging, a continuity of belonging with everything else. You have this contact. You have this contact with Israel. You have this contact with the poor person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a lot people feel like me. Because otherwise if we raise-- I mean an organization like this, close to $150,00, $200,000 a year. Able to be together, see each other. My greatest joy is to come to some of those affairs, and see-- I'll use a Jewish word, [NON-ENGLISH]. People-- the survivor's average age is now middle 70s almost, early 70s. To be on the dance floor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be able to dance and get dressed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and see each other. Because you can't see 700 people on a daily basis. Then we have, of course, the misfortune of going to funerals, and that's part of life. And we go to funerals too often nowadays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the message is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] learn from past. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Think about it. Read about it. It's a fact. It's not a make-believe. We didn't make up those stories. I'm one individual from a family of 80, 90 people, who survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have preferred to live in my little town, Tomaszow, with my family, not have this beautiful home, not drive cars, and live my life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were forced into this situation by a maniac named Hitler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the whole world stood by and let him do it, and didn't stop it. There's no excuse. I happen to have gone this February to a 50th reunion, Holocaust reunion in Miami Beach. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was just 3,000 survivors, but there's still 3,000 survivors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a speaker-- of course the main speaker was Elie Wiesel, and the other, representative of the United States was Secretary of Defense Perry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Wiesel asked, Mr. Secretary, it's 50 years since the war ended. You have entrance to-- to the archives. Can you tell us why the Allieds never bombed the railroads or the crematoriums? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were aware. So please, whoever who watches this, when you are aware, do something about it. Don't ignore it. It might happen to you. It might happen to your kids, or it might happen three generations from you. So whichever person [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] can do the minute-- and sometimes we say what can one person do? But we have seen that the influence of one individual is very great. Because if this individual appeals to two people, and the other two people, and two other people-- so please, do the best that you possibly can. That's the advice. And be a good person. Thank you very much for sharing your testimony with us. We appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture came from my uncle. It was a little tiny picture. Because I had no pictures. I have no-- nothing left from-- but I had a surviving uncle who lived in France. And eventually, I brought him to United States. So it was a tiny picture made by my uncle in Tomaszow, who I-- I told about it, who a photographer. And my wife made a portrait from that big picture. And the people in it from my right, my sister, Faiga; my brother, Pesach; myself, the little fellow; Simcha, and then my father, Abraham, and my mama, Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was done in Tomaszow, Poland, probably 1935, I would say, approximately. This picture was done in Germany after the liberation, about the beginning of 1946. I was about 17 years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From the left to the right, this is Morris Pearl, that I told you about and [? Tamara, ?] his wife, maiden name [? Rosenblatt. ?] And this is the man that, I guess, I owe a lot because his influence made me come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were taken also in Germany in about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] end of '45. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My name is Sam Steinberg again. And this is a picture at my wedding day. I was a whole 18 years old, not quite 18. I was missing two-- three days. And anyway, that was my wedding picture. It was done here in 1948, in August, and it was done in Los Angeles here. And of course, this is my wedding picture. Again, we were married on August 28, 1948-- my dear wife, Selma, who was born in Austria, and was lucky enough to come in 1939 over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in-- when we're-- wedding-- made August 28, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. OK. This in my whole mispucha, my family. And we're going from right to left. We'll start with the top, the people standing up. It's John Kerr, which is the husband of my daughter, then Lisa Steinberg, who is the wife of Perry. Then it's Perry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son. And Karen Steinberg, who is the wife of my son, Steven Steinberg, next. And Arthur Gordon who is the husband of my daughter, Rachel. Then we'll go from left to right. Joseph Gordon, who is my grandson; Sarah Gordon, who's the oldest; and Laura Gordon. Then Rachel Gordon, who is my daughter. And we have Andrea Steinberg, who is the daughter of Steven, and then myself, and my name is Sam. And Erica, who is also the daughter of Steven. Then Selma who is my wife, and Jacqueline who's the daughter of Perry, my youngest son. And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Maggie, who's-- who's the daughter of Sharon, my daughter, and then Sharon, and then Gwen, which is the daughter of Sharon. And that's all there is. We're kaynahorah, 18 of us. And of course, myself and Selma are just-- we're in there. We said before. Thank you.
